Notice Of Motion And Motion To Amend His First Amended Complaint; Declaration; And, Memorandum; **Court Reporter Fees Paid**
Off Calendar Dec-23-2021 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO'S Motion To Amend His First Amended Complaint is denied. While there is a liberal policy to hear cases on the merits and allow amendment, it is not an abuse of discretion to deny amendment where the plaintiff delayed presenting the proposed amendment/s and that delay prejudices the defendant. (See Melican v. Regents of University of California (2007) 151 Cal.App.4th 168, 175.) Here, the court concludes that plaintiff unreasonably delayed seeking amendment and allowing amendment at this stage of the proceedings would prejudice defendant. Plaintiff concedes that he learned of the facts regarding Eisen (who is repeatedly named in the FAC) on August 23, 2021, but he waited until November 22, 2021 to file the instant motion. (DiGiacomo Decl., par. 10.) With regard to the other proposed amendments, plaintiff does not sufficiently explain why amendment is necessary and proper now, which violates Cal. R. Ct. 3.1345. Permitting amendment at this juncture would prejudice defendants since the amendment would moot the pending summary judgment motion and defendant will not have sufficient time to propound supplemental discovery regarding the new claims in light of the trial date of January 18, 2022. On December 2, 2021, the Presiding Judge decided that the trial date should remain on that date so a continuance to dampen the effect of the prejudice is not possible. The motion is therefore denied.

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Not Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER to Amended ANSWER to Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-09-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Vincent DiGiacomo (415-595-4107; raydigiacomojr@gmail.com) Plaintiff in propria persona.
No other appearances.

DISCUSSED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript) re: clarification. The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO's DEMURRER to Amended ANSWER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT:

Plaintiff Raymond DiGiacomo's demurrer to the second amended answer (SAA) is sustained with leave to amend in part and overruled in part. Plaintiff's demurrer for failure to state sufficient facts to constitute an affirmative defense is sustained. Facts in affirmative defenses must be "averred as carefully and with as much detail as the facts which constitute the cause of action and are alleged in the complaint." (FPI Development, Inc. v. Nakashima (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 367, 384.) The SAA's laundry-list of 34 affirmative defenses does not meet the required standard. Rather, defendants use the same duplicated facts in almost every defense, where the facts largely fail to relate to or state a defense.

Plaintiff's remaining arguments are not persuasive. A demurrer may not be sustained to a portion of a cause of action or a defense. (See Daniels v. Select Portfolio Servicing, Inc. (2016) 246 Cal.App.4th 1150, 1167.) Further, a "demurrer for uncertainty is strictly construed, even where a complaint is in some respects uncertain, because ambiguities can be clarified under modern discovery procedures." (Khoury v. Maly's of California (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 616.) Defendant identifies no ambiguities that cannot be clarified in discovery.

Order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER to Amended ANSWER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-10-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO's Demurrer to Amended Answer to 1st Amended Complaint.

No appearance on this matter. The tentative ruling is adopted as follows: Plaintiff's demurrer to amended answer is off calendar. Neither the declaration filed on January 4, 2021 nor the filings of February 3, 2021, demonstrates that the parties met and conferred "in person or by telephone" in compliance with Code of Civil Procedure section 430.41(a). The Court orders the parties to meet and confer if plaintiff intends to renew his attack on the amended answer. For the parties' guidance, the Court notes the following: (1) defendants' 55 affirmative defenses seem excessive; (2) an affirmative defense must be pleaded with the same care as a cause of action (see FPI Development, Inc. v. Nakashima (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 367, 384); (3) a demurrer assumes the truth of the allegations and a demurrer cannot rely on extrinsic evidence to challenge the allegations (see Ion Equipment Corp. v. Nelson (1980) 110 Cal.App.3d 868, 881; Lance Camper Manufacturing Corp. v. Republic Indem. Co. (1996) 44 Cal.App.4th 194, 198); (4) plaintiff is not required to respond to defendants' amended answer; and (5) while a demurrer to an answer is a permissible filing, it generally does very little to move the litigation forward.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda; Not Reported (302/EPS)
